source_metadata.title_raw: Michael Torres - A-18-026 - March 14, 2018 - Newport Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 14, 2018

Michael Torres
Assistant City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-026

Dear Mr. Torres:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach City Planning
Commissioner Peter Zak regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions
of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict
of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Commissioner Zak take part in decisions relating to a residential development project
given that he has an ownership interest in a development company that leases office space across
the street from the project area?

CONCLUSION

No. Commissioner Zak may not take part in decisions related to the project because he has a
disqualifying conflict of interest due to his interest in his business partner as a source of income.

_______________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-026
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

Commissioner Zak was appointed to the Newport Beach City Planning Commission (the
"Planning Commission") in June 2015, and he currently serves as the Planning Commission's Vice
Chairman.

Commissioner Zak has a 22.5% ownership interest in the Lyon Living Development
Company (the "Development Company"), in which he has invested over $2,000. Commissioner
Zak is the Development Company's manager, president, and chief operating officer, as well as a
member of the Development Company's executive committee. The Development Company
provides a wide range of residential real estate services, including but not limited to, property
development, construction, operations, and asset management.

The Development Company leases approximately 30% of the commercial office space in a
building located at 4901 Birch Street under a long-term lease. The Development Company does not
sublease the office space, nor does it own or manage residential real estate in Newport Beach.

Commissioner Zak's business partner owns the building located at 4901 Birch Street, as
well as a 50% stake in the Development Company. Commissioner Zak's business partner also owns
two commercial buildings in the same area, located at 4911 Birch Street and 4650 Von Karman
Drive.

Shopoff Land Fund II, L.P. submitted a development application for commercial property
across the street from 4901 Birch Street, roughly 278 feet away. Specifically, the application
proposes development of three residential 13-story condominium structures and a parking garage on
roughly 12.5 acres (the "Project"). The Project's proposed location is surrounded to the northwest
by two hotels, the northeast and south by office buildings, and to the southeast by office buildings,
fast food restaurants, a superior courthouse, and a commercial-residential mixed-use project. The
Project will bring high residential density to a predominantly commercial area. The Draft
Environmental Impact Report (the "EIR") concluded that the Project may result in significant
impacts, even after mitigation measures are imposed, particularly with respect to noise and air
quality. The Project is visible from 4901 Birch Street.

The Project's development plans are tentatively scheduled to come before the Planning
Commission in early 2018. Since his appointment to the Planning Commission, Commissioner Zak
has recused himself from participating in any items related to the Project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Specific to your facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise, including:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

File No. A-18-026
Page No. 3

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b)).²

• An interest in a source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Regulation 18700.1(a)(2)(A) provides that, in addition to having a financial interest in any business entity from which the official has received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the relevant decision is made, the official also has an interest in any individual owning a 50% or greater interest in that business entity.

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

You indicated that Commissioner Zak has invested more than $2,000 in the Development Company and is a partner, manager, and chief operating officer. It also appears that Commissioner Zak receives income from the Development Company that will equal or exceed $500 in the 12 months prior to the decision. In addition, the Development Company leases a commercial office space. Therefore, Commissioner Zak has interests in the Development Company as both a business entity and source of income, a leasehold real property interest in 4901 Birch Street, and an interest in his personal finances. Moreover, because his business partner has a 50% or greater interest in the Development Company, Commissioner Zak also has an interest in his business partner as a source of income under Regulation 18700.1(a)(2)(A).

Foreseability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision, and is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).)³ For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Commissioner Zak's interests are not explicitly involved in the Planning Commission's decisions regarding the Project's development application.

Source of Income

With respect to sources of income who are individuals not explicitly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the interest is material if the individual, "will be financially affected under the standards applied to an

_______________
2 "Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, except periodic tenancies of one month or less. (Section 82033; Regulation 18233.)

3 A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

File No. A-18-026
Page No. 4

official in Regulation 18702.5, the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has
an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially affected under the standards
applied to a financial interest in Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.2, respectively." Therefore, to
determine whether there is a conflict with respect to Commissioner Zak's interest in his business
partner as a source of income, we must analyze whether his business partner has a business or real
property interest that will be financially affected under the standards found in Regulations 18702.1
or 18702.2.

Real Property Owned by Commissioner Zak's Business Partner

Specific to your facts, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect
of a decision on a real property interest is material if the effect:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of
the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

[¶]…[¶]

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was
of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market
value of the official's property."

You provided that the Project is expected to bring high residential density to a
predominantly commercial area. The increase in residents in the area may increase the intensity of
use of city streets and infrastructure in the immediate area surrounding the Project, which includes
Commissioner Zak's business partner's three properties located in close proximity to the Project. In
addition, you noted that the EIR concluded that the Project may cause significant noise and air
quality impacts in the Project's surrounding area, even after mitigation measures. You also stated
that the Project's three residential 13-story condominium buildings would be visible from 4901
Birch Street, which would also alter the property's view. As a result, it is reasonably foreseeable
that the Project will change the character of the parcel of real property and will influence its market
value. Therefore, the Project will have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on Commissioner
Zak's business partner's real property interests and, by extension, Commissioner Zak's financial
interest in his business partner.

Business Entity Interest Owned by Commissioner Zak's Business Partner

Based on your facts, Commissioner Zak's business partner utilizes 4901 Birch Street as a
commercial office enterprise, which qualifies as a "business entity" under Section 82005.
Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business entity
not explicitly involved in a decision is material if a prudent person with sufficient information
would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the
value of the privately held business interest. Examples of decisions that may affect the value of a
business entity

File No. A-18-026
Page No. 5

privately held business entity include decisions that result in improvements in the surrounding
neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic or road improvements, or parking changes
that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity
receives. (Regulation 18702.1(b)(4).)

According to your facts, there will be a significant impact on noise and air quality and an
alteration in intensity of use, which may contribute to a change in the value of Commissioner Zak's
business partner's business entity that leases commercial office space because the office space may
become more or less desirable, depending on the customer, as a result of the Project's significant
impacts. Therefore, a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the Project's financial effect (whether negative or positive) would contribute to a
change in the business entity's value at any of the three commercial properties located in close
proximity to the Project.

As we conclude that Commissioner Zak has a conflict of interest based on his source-of-
income interest in his business partner, we do not analyze his other financial interests.

"Public Generally" Exception

The Act allows a public official to make and participate in governmental decisions affecting
an official's interests if the effect is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (See
Section 87103; Regulation 18703(a).) Specifically, Regulation 18703(a) provides that an effect on
an official's interest is "indistinguishable" from its effect on the public generally if a significant
segment of the public is affected, and the effect on the interest is not unique when compared to the
effect on the significant segment of the public.⁴

Due to the Project's close proximity of all three of Commissioner Zak's business partner's
properties and the commercial office rental business, the material financial effect on Commissioner
Zak is unique and distinguishable from a significant segment of the public.

Therefore, under the Act, Commissioner Zak may not participate in the Planning
Commission's decision on the Project's development application as his source-of-income interest in
his business partner will result in a reasonably foreseeable material effect on his business partner's
real property and business entity interests.⁵

⁴ A significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of: (1) all businesses or nonprofit entities within the
official's jurisdiction; (2) all real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's
jurisdiction; or (3) all individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).)

⁵ Please note that when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest
on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. A-18-026
Page No. 6

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

more. (Section 87103(b).)2

months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Regulation 18700.1 (a)(2)(A) provides that,
